abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable prowess in
generating contextually coherent responses, yet their fixed context windows
pose fundamental challenges for maintaining consistency over prolonged
multi-session dialogues. We introduce Mem0, a scalable memory-centric
architecture that addresses this issue by dynamically extracting,
consolidating, and retrieving salient information from ongoing conversations.
Building on this foundation, we further propose an enhanced variant that
leverages graph-based memory representations to capture complex relational
structures among conversational elements. Through comprehensive evaluations on
LOCOMO benchmark, we systematically compare our approaches against six baseline
categories: (i) established memory-augmented systems, (ii) retrieval-augmented
generation (RAG) with varying chunk sizes and k-values, (iii) a full-context
approach that processes the entire conversation history, (iv) an open-source
memory solution, (v) a proprietary model system, and (vi) a dedicated memory
management platform. Empirical results show that our methods consistently
outperform all existing memory systems across four question categories:
single-hop, temporal, multi-hop, and open-domain. Notably, Mem0 achieves 26%
relative improvements in the LLM-as-a-Judge metric over OpenAI, while Mem0 with
graph memory achieves around 2% higher overall score than the base
configuration. Beyond accuracy gains, we also markedly reduce computational
overhead compared to full-context method. In particular, Mem0 attains a 91%
lower p95 latency and saves more than 90% token cost, offering a compelling
balance between advanced reasoning capabilities and practical deployment
constraints. Our findings highlight critical role of structured, persistent
memory mechanisms for long-term conversational coherence, paving the way for
more reliable and efficient LLM-driven AI agents.

keyInsights: Integrating LLMs with external memory requires dynamic extraction and consolidation of salient information rather than storing raw text or complex summaries.
Graph-based memory (Mem0g) effectively captures complex relational structures, significantly improving performance on temporal reasoning tasks.
A practical balance between memory accuracy and deployment metrics (latency, token consumption) is crucial for production-ready AI agents.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) have fixed context windows, causing them to "forget" past interactions and struggle with long-term conversational coherence and consistency across multi-session dialogues.

Existing solutions like extended context windows or basic Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) often delay or partially address the problem, lacking the specificity and nuance for robust long-term memory.
The lack of persistent memory prevents AI agents from maintaining user preferences, building continuous narratives, and performing advanced reasoning over extended interactions.
results: Mem0 and Mem0g consistently outperform most RAG configurations and open-source memory solutions, with Mem0g achieving an overall LLM-as-a-Judge score of 68.44%.
Mem0g demonstrated superior performance in temporal reasoning (J=58.13%), outperforming all baselines, and was competitive in open-domain scenarios.

Mem0 achieved 91% lower p95 total latency (1.440s) compared to full-context processing (17.117s) and significantly lower token consumption than other memory providers like Zep (7k tokens vs. over 600k tokens).
solution: The paper introduces Mem0, a scalable long-term memory architecture with an extraction phase (LLM identifies salient memories) and an update phase (LLM determines ADD, UPDATE, DELETE, NOOP operations on memories).
Mem0g extends Mem0 by incorporating graph-based memory, storing information as entities and relationships using a two-stage LLM pipeline for entity extraction and relationship generation.
Memory retrieval in Mem0g utilizes both entity-centric exploration of subgraphs and semantic triplet matching for efficient access to relevant information.
paper_summary.summary: Mem0 and Mem0g offer scalable long-term memory architectures for AI agents, enabling them to overcome LLM context window limitations by dynamically extracting, consolidating, and retrieving salient information. These solutions significantly enhance conversational coherence and reasoning capabilities while achieving substantial reductions in latency and token consumption compared to existing methods.

title: Mem0: Building Production-Ready AI Agents with Scalable Long-Term Memory

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents face fundamental limitations in long-horizon reasoning due to finite context windows, making effective memory management critical. Existing methods typically handle long-term memory (LTM) and short-term memory (STM) as separate components, relying on heuristics or auxiliary controllers, which limits adaptability and end-to-end optimization. In this paper, we propose Agentic Memory (AgeMem), a unified framework that integrates LTM and STM management directly into the agent's policy. AgeMem exposes memory operations as tool-based actions, enabling the LLM agent to autonomously decide what and when to store, retrieve, update, summarize, or discard information. To train such unified behaviors, we propose a three-stage progressive reinforcement learning strategy and design a step-wise GRPO to address sparse and discontinuous rewards induced by memory operations. Experiments on five long-horizon benchmarks demonstrate that AgeMem consistently outperforms strong memory-augmented baselines across multiple LLM backbones, achieving improved task performance, higher-quality long-term memory, and more efficient context usage.

keyInsights: Unifying LTM and STM management within the agent's policy, rather than treating them as separate, external modules, significantly improves long-horizon reasoning.
Treating memory operations as explicit, learnable tools empowers the LLM agent to adapt its memory strategy based on task demands and learned experience.
A progressive RL strategy with long-range credit assignment effectively overcomes the challenges of sparse and discontinuous rewards inherent in optimizing memory-related actions.
originalProblem: LLM agents are fundamentally limited by finite context windows, hindering their ability to perform long-horizon reasoning tasks.
Existing memory management approaches treat long-term (LTM) and short-term (STM) memories as independent, loosely coupled modules, leading to fragmented and suboptimal performance.
Current methods for memory control often rely on handcrafted rules or auxiliary expert models, increasing inference costs and limiting adaptability.
results: AgeMem achieved the highest average performance on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (41.96%) and Qwen3-4B-Instruct (54.31%), outperforming memory-augmented baselines by 4.82 to 8.57 percentage points.
RL training yielded substantial improvements of 8.53 and 8.72 percentage points over the non-RL version, validating the progressive RL strategy's effectiveness.
AgeMem demonstrated higher Memory Quality (MQ) scores (0.533 for Qwen2.5, 0.605 for Qwen3) on HotpotQA and reduced prompt token usage by 3.1% to 5.1% compared to RAG-based counterparts.
solution: AgeMem formulates memory management as an intrinsic, learnable part of the agent's policy using a unified reinforcement learning (RL) framework.
Memory operations (e.g., store, retrieve, summarize, filter) are exposed as tool-based actions that the LLM agent can autonomously invoke.

A three-stage progressive RL strategy, combined with step-wise Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), is used to train the agent to learn stable and unified memory behaviors across different timescales, addressing sparse and discontinuous rewards.
paper_summary.summary: AgeMem introduces a framework for Large Language Model (LLM) agents that unifies long-term and short-term memory management through a learnable policy, enabling the agent to autonomously control memory operations. This approach improved average task performance across diverse benchmarks by 4.82 to 8.57 percentage points over existing memory-augmented baselines and enhanced memory quality and context efficiency.

title: Agentic Memory: Learning Unified Long-Term and Short-Term Memory Management for Large Language Model Agents

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have evolved to intelligently process information, make decisions, and interact with users or tools. A key capability is the integration of long-term memory capabilities, enabling these agents to draw upon historical interactions and knowledge. However, the growing memory size and need for semantic structuring pose significant challenges. In this work, we propose an autonomous memory augmentation approach, MemInsight, to enhance semantic data representation and retrieval mechanisms. By leveraging autonomous augmentation to historical interactions, LLM agents are shown to deliver more accurate and contextualized responses. We empirically validate the efficacy of our proposed approach in three task scenarios; conversational recommendation, question answering and event summarization. On the LLM-REDIAL dataset, MemInsight boosts persuasiveness of recommendations by up to 14%. Moreover, it outperforms a RAG baseline by 34% in recall for LoCoMo retrieval. Our empirical results show the potential of MemInsight to enhance the contextual performance of LLM agents across multiple tasks.

keyInsights: LLMs can autonomously identify and extract structured, semantically meaningful attribute-value pairs from historical interactions, enabling dynamic adaptation without human intervention.
Augmenting memory instances with explicit attributes provides richer semantic representations, which can be prioritized (Priority Augmentation) to improve retrieval effectiveness.

Combining attribute-based and embedding-based retrieval mechanisms, guided by these autonomous augmentations, leads to more precise and contextually relevant information access than traditional methods.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent memory systems struggle with scalability as historical data rapidly grows, making efficient storage and retrieval difficult.
Unstructured or semi-structured memory often contains noise and irrelevant details, hindering effective retrieval and degrading agent performance.
Limitations in context window size and reliance on manually defined schemas for structured memory restrict an agent's adaptability and ability to integrate knowledge across diverse tasks.
results: MemInsight achieved superior accuracy in question answering, with priority augmentation yielding a 35% overall improvement in recall (60.5% vs 26.5% for DPR) and substantial gains on multi-hop questions.

In conversational recommendation, embedding-based retrieval led to a 12% increase in 'highly persuasive' recommendations, while attribute-based filtering maintained comparable performance with significantly less retrieved memory.

It demonstrated comparable or improved event summarization quality, particularly with turn-level augmentations, and achieved a 99.14% factual consistency rate for generated annotations, indicating high reliability.
solution: A three-module autonomous memory augmentation framework (Attribute Mining, Annotation, and Memory Retrieval) leveraging an LLM backbone for self-structuring and intelligent retrieval.

Attribute Mining extracts structured, semantically meaningful attributes from input dialogues, differentiating between entity-centric and conversation-centric perspectives at turn or session granularity.

Memory Retrieval utilizes these augmentations through either attribute-based filtering (matching attributes) or embedding-based similarity search (vector similarity) to retrieve contextually relevant information.
paper_summary.summary: MemInsight, developed by AWS AI, introduces an autonomous framework to enhance Large Language Model (LLM) agents' long-term memory by automatically extracting and utilizing semantically meaningful attributes from historical interactions. This approach improves information retrieval and agent performance across question answering, conversational recommendation, and event summarization tasks.

title: MemInsight: Autonomous Memory Augmentation for LLM Agents

abstract: Despite their remarkable capabilities, Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to effectively leverage historical interaction information in dynamic and complex environments. Memory systems enable LLMs to move beyond stateless interactions by introducing persistent information storage, retrieval, and utilization mechanisms. However, existing memory systems often introduce substantial time and computational overhead. To this end, we introduce a new memory system called LightMem, which strikes a balance between the performance and efficiency of memory systems. Inspired by the Atkinson-Shiffrin model of human memory, LightMem organizes memory into three complementary stages. First, cognition-inspired sensory memory rapidly filters irrelevant information through lightweight compression and groups information according to their topics. Next, topic-aware short-term memory consolidates these topic-based groups, organizing and summarizing content for more structured access. Finally, long-term memory with sleep-time update employs an offline procedure that decouples consolidation from online inference. On LongMemEval and LoCoMo, using GPT and Qwen backbones, LightMem consistently surpasses strong baselines, improving QA accuracy by up to 7.7% / 29.3%, reducing total token usage by up to 38x / 20.9x and API calls by up to 30x / 55.5x, while purely online test-time costs are even lower, achieving up to 106x / 117x token reduction and 159x / 310x fewer API calls. The code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: A multi-stage, cognition-inspired memory architecture significantly improves both the efficiency and effectiveness of LLM agents in dynamic, long-context environments.

Decoupling expensive memory updates from online inference, through a 'sleep-time' update mechanism, dramatically reduces real-time latency and enables deeper, parallel processing of historical information.
Pre-compressing raw input and topic-aware segmentation efficiently reduce redundant information and improve the semantic coherence of memory units without sacrificing model accuracy.
originalProblem: Existing LLM memory systems often process redundant raw interaction data, leading to high computational overhead and increased token usage.
Ineffective short-term memory management fails to capture semantic connections across turns, resulting in inaccurate or incomplete memory representations.
Memory updates and forgetting mechanisms are typically coupled with real-time inference, causing significant latency during online interactions.
results: LightMem achieved up to a 117.1x reduction in online token consumption and up to a 309.9x reduction in API calls on benchmarks like LONGMEMEVAL, significantly outperforming baselines.
The system demonstrated improved task accuracy, with gains ranging from 2.09% to 29.29% on conversational memory benchmarks (LONGMEMEVAL and LOCOMO) across different LLM backbones.
Overall runtime was accelerated by up to 12.4x for GPT and 6.3x for Qwen models, showcasing substantial efficiency gains during memory bank construction and usage.
solution: A Sensory Memory module rapidly filters irrelevant information using pre-compression and groups content into topic-based segments through hybrid topic segmentation.
A Topic-Aware Short-Term Memory module consolidates these segments, generating concise, accurate summaries while minimizing API calls by triggering summarization only when a buffer threshold is met.

A Long-Term Memory module employs 'soft' online updates and an 'offline parallel update' mechanism (sleep-time update) to decouple memory consolidation from real-time inference, reducing latency and enabling reflective processing.
paper_summary.summary: LightMem, developed by Zhejiang University, presents a cognition-inspired, multi-stage memory system for large language models to efficiently manage long-term conversational history. It reduces online token consumption by up to 117.1x and API calls by up to 309.9x, while improving task accuracy by up to 29.29% on interactive memory benchmarks.

title: LightMem: Lightweight and Efficient Memory-Augmented Generation

abstract: To support long-term interaction in complex environments, LLM agents require memory systems that manage historical experiences. Existing approaches either retain full interaction histories via passive context extension, leading to substantial redundancy, or rely on iterative reasoning to filter noise, incurring high token costs. To address this challenge, we introduce SimpleMem, an efficient memory framework based on semantic lossless compression. We propose a three-stage pipeline designed to maximize information density and token utilization: (1) Semantic Structured Compression, which distills unstructured interactions into compact, multi-view indexed memory units; (2) Online Semantic Synthesis, an intra-session process that instantly integrates related context into unified abstract representations to eliminate redundancy; and (3) Intent-Aware Retrieval Planning, which infers search intent to dynamically determine retrieval scope and construct precise context efficiently. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that our method consistently outperforms baseline approaches in accuracy, retrieval efficiency, and inference cost, achieving an average F1 improvement of 26.4% in LoCoMo while reducing inference-time token consumption by up to 30-fold, demonstrating a superior balance between performance and efficiency. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Efficient lifelong memory for LLM agents can be achieved by actively filtering noise and semantically compressing information, rather than merely extending context windows.
Structuring memory with multi-view indexing and recursive consolidation allows for scalable storage and retrieval of both fine-grained details and high-level abstract representations.
Dynamically adapting memory retrieval based on query complexity is crucial for maximizing token utilization and maintaining performance under strict efficiency constraints.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with fixed context windows, limiting their long-term memory and ability to retain information across extended interactions.
Memory systems for LLM agents suffer from context inflation due to redundant, low-entropy dialogue, which degrades effective information density and model performance.
Current approaches for augmenting LLM memory incur substantial computational overhead, including high token costs and increased latency, hindering practical deployment.
results: Achieved a 26.4% average F1 score improvement over strong baselines like Mem0 on the LoCoMo benchmark.
Reduced inference-time token consumption by up to 30x compared to full-context methods and 40-50% against other optimized baselines, significantly lowering operational costs.
Demonstrated superior efficiency with 14x faster memory construction and approximately 33% faster retrieval latency compared to leading baselines.
Enabled smaller LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-1.5b) to achieve competitive performance, outperforming larger models using less efficient memory strategies.
solution: A three-stage memory pipeline that includes semantic structured compression, multi-view structured indexing with recursive consolidation, and adaptive query-aware retrieval.
Implements entropy-aware filtering to remove redundant dialogue and normalizes informative content into self-contained, context-independent memory units.
Utilizes multi-layered indexing (semantic, lexical, symbolic) and an asynchronous background process for recursive consolidation, creating higher-level abstract memory representations.
Dynamically adjusts the number of retrieved memory entries based on an estimated query complexity, balancing context relevance and token efficiency.
paper_summary.summary: SimpleMem presents an efficient lifelong memory framework for LLM agents, utilizing semantic structured compression and adaptive retrieval to filter redundancy and consolidate experiences. The system achieves an average F1 score improvement of 26.4% while reducing inference-time token consumption by up to 30x and speeding up memory construction 14x compared to existing methods.

title: SimpleMem: Efficient Lifelong Memory for LLM Agents

abstract: Modern language agents must operate over long-horizon, multi-turn interactions, where they retrieve external information, adapt to observations, and answer interdependent queries. Yet, most LLM systems rely on full-context prompting, appending all past turns regardless of their relevance. This leads to unbounded memory growth, increased computational costs, and degraded reasoning performance on out-of-distribution input lengths. We introduce MEM1, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that enables agents to operate with constant memory across long multi-turn tasks. At each turn, MEM1 updates a compact shared internal state that jointly supports memory consolidation and reasoning. This state integrates prior memory with new observations from the environment while strategically discarding irrelevant or redundant information. To support training in more realistic and compositional settings, we propose a simple yet effective and scalable approach to constructing multi-turn environments by composing existing datasets into arbitrarily complex task sequences. Experiments across three domains, including internal retrieval QA, open-domain web QA, and multi-turn web shopping, show that MEM1-7B improves performance by 3.5x while reducing memory usage by 3.7x compared to Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct on a 16-objective multi-hop QA task, and generalizes beyond the training horizon. Our results demonstrate the promise of reasoning-driven memory consolidation as a scalable alternative to existing solutions for training long-horizon interactive agents, where both efficiency and performance are optimized.

keyInsights: Inference-time reasoning can be synergized with memory consolidation, allowing the agent to learn to extract and retain key information while discarding irrelevant details.
Reinforcement learning, when combined with context pruning, naturally incentivizes the agent to develop effective internal memory management strategies.
Composing existing single-objective datasets into multi-objective tasks creates effective long-horizon benchmarks for training and evaluating memory-efficient agents.
originalProblem: Unbounded context growth in multi-turn LLM interactions leads to exploding computational costs and memory usage (O(N^2) or O(N)).
LLMs trained on short contexts struggle to generalize to longer, out-of-distribution input lengths, causing performance degradation.
Accumulating irrelevant information in the context overloads the model's attention, hindering reasoning and leading to the "lost in the middle" problem.
results: Maintains an almost constant peak token count on multi-objective QA tasks while other methods show linear growth, achieving up to 72.9% reduction in peak tokens.
Surpasses larger LLMs (e.g., Qwen2.5-14B-Instruct) in performance on complex 8- and 16-objective QA tasks, showing better generalization to long horizons.
Achieves the highest average final reward (70.87) on WebShop navigation with significantly lower resource usage (2.8x lower peak tokens, 1.5x faster inference) compared to baselines.
solution: Introduces a compact shared internal state (<IS>) that iteratively consolidates prior memory with new observations, becoming the agent's only retained memory.
Prunes all previous turn context, tool outputs, and observations after consolidation, maintaining constant memory usage across turns.
Trains the agent end-to-end using reinforcement learning (PPO) where task success incentivizes the agent to learn memory consolidation intrinsically.
paper_summary.summary: MEM1 proposes an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that enables Large Language Model agents to maintain constant memory usage while operating over long-horizon, multi-turn interactions. This framework, from researchers at SMART/MIT, demonstrates superior efficiency and competitive or improved performance on multi-objective QA and interactive navigation tasks.

title: MEM1: Learning to Synergize Memory and Reasoning for Efficient Long-Horizon Agents

abstract:
Recent large language model (LLM)-driven chat assistant systems have
integrated memory components to track user-assistant chat histories, enabling
more accurate and personalized responses. However, their long-term memory
capabilities in sustained interactions remain underexplored. We introduce
LongMemEval, a comprehensive benchmark designed to evaluate five core long-term
memory abilities of chat assistants: information extraction, multi-session
reasoning, temporal reasoning, knowledge updates, and abstention. With 500
meticulously curated questions embedded within freely scalable user-assistant
chat histories, LongMemEval presents a significant challenge to existing
long-term memory systems, with commercial chat assistants and long-context LLMs
showing a 30% accuracy drop on memorizing information across sustained
interactions. We then present a unified framework that breaks down the
long-term memory design into three stages: indexing, retrieval, and reading.
Built upon key experimental insights, we propose several memory design
optimizations including session decomposition for value granularity,
fact-augmented key expansion for indexing, and time-aware query expansion for
refining the search scope. Extensive experiments show that these optimizations
greatly improve both memory recall and downstream question answering on
LongMemEval. Overall, our study provides valuable resources and guidance for
advancing the long-term memory capabilities of LLM-based chat assistants,
paving the way toward more personalized and reliable conversational AI. Our
benchmark and code are publicly available at
this https URL

keyInsights: Current long-context LLMs and commercial chat assistants still struggle with long-term, multi-session interactions, even with large context windows, exhibiting substantial performance drops.
Effective long-term memory systems for chat assistants require optimization across all stages: indexing, retrieval, and reading, rather than relying solely on larger context windows.

Specific design choices, such as using granular 'round' values, fact-augmented key expansion, time-aware queries, and Chain-of-Note with structured JSON for reading, empirically improve memory recall and question answering.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) lack the ability to retain and leverage long-term personal knowledge across sustained, multi-session user-AI interactions, leading to diminished accuracy and user satisfaction.

Existing benchmarks for long-term dialogue understanding are limited in realism, context length scalability (often restricted to a few thousand tokens), and comprehensive coverage of dynamic memory abilities (e.g., multi-session reasoning, knowledge updates, temporal reasoning, assistant-provided information recall, and abstention).

Current commercial chat assistants and state-of-the-art long-context LLMs struggle to effectively manage and reason over extensive interaction histories, indicating a gap in genuine long-term memory capability.
results: Commercial memory-augmented chat assistants like ChatGPT and Coze showed substantial performance drops (37% to 64%) compared to an LLM directly reading full context on a simplified version of LONGMEMEVAL.

State-of-the-art long-context LLMs experienced 30% to 60% performance declines on LONGMEMEVAL_S (115k tokens) when processing the entire history, compared to an oracle setting, indicating persistent 'lost-in-the-middle' phenomena.

Fact-augmented key expansion improved Recall@k by an average of 9.4% and end-to-end QA accuracy by 5.4%, while time-aware query expansion improved temporal reasoning recall by 6.8% to 11.3%; combining Chain-of-Note with structured JSON format also consistently improved QA accuracy.
solution: Introduced LONGMEMEVAL, a novel benchmark designed to comprehensively evaluate five core long-term memory abilities (Information Extraction, Multi-Session Reasoning, Knowledge Updates, Temporal Reasoning, Abstention) across coherent chat histories up to 1.5 million tokens.

Developed a unified three-stage (indexing, retrieval, reading) and four-control-point (value, key, query, reading strategy) framework for systematically analyzing and designing memory-augmented chat assistants.

Conducted extensive empirical evaluations using LONGMEMEVAL to identify and validate practical design optimizations for memory systems, providing actionable guidance for enhancing long-term memory capabilities in LLM-based assistants.
paper_summary.summary: The paper presents LONGMEMEVAL, a new benchmark for evaluating long-term interactive memory in chat assistants, featuring scalable histories up to 1.5 million tokens and covering five core memory abilities. It also proposes a unified framework for designing memory-augmented systems and demonstrates that commercial assistants and long-context LLMs struggle with long-term memory, while optimizing value granularity, key expansion, time-aware querying, and reading strategies can improve performance.

title: LongMemEval: Benchmarking Chat Assistants on Long-Term Interactive  Memory

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are constrained by limited context windows, necessitating external memory systems for long-term information understanding. Current memory-augmented agents typically depend on pre-defined instructions and tools for memory updates. However, language models may lack the ability to determine which information to store, how to structure it, and when to update it, especially as memory systems become more complex. This results in suboptimal memory construction and information loss. To this end, we propose Mem-alpha, a reinforcement learning framework that trains agents to effectively manage complex memory systems through interaction and feedback. We also construct a specialized training dataset spanning diverse multi-turn interaction patterns paired with comprehensive evaluation questions designed to teach effective memory management. During training, agents process sequential information chunks, learn to extract and store relevant content, then update the memory system. The reward signal derives from downstream question-answering accuracy over the full interaction history, directly optimizing for memory construction. To illustrate the effectiveness of our training framework, we design a memory architecture comprising core, episodic, and semantic components, equipped with multiple tools for memory operations. Empirical evaluation demonstrates that Mem-alpha achieves significant improvements over existing memory-augmented agent baselines. Despite being trained exclusively on instances with a maximum length of 30k tokens, our agents exhibit remarkable generalization to sequences exceeding 400k tokens, over 13x the training length, highlighting the robustness of Mem-alpha.

keyInsights: Reinforcement learning effectively enables LLM agents to learn adaptive and robust memory management strategies for complex, multi-component external memory systems, moving beyond instruction-following limitations.

Directly optimizing memory construction for downstream task performance via RL leads to substantially better information retention and more efficient memory utilization compared to instruction-based or rule-based approaches.

The learned memory management principles generalize remarkably well to contexts significantly longer than those encountered during training, indicating a fundamental understanding rather than rote memorization.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are constrained by limited context windows, hindering their ability to process and retain information over long interactions.

Existing external memory systems for LLMs rely on predefined instructions or heuristics, which often lead to suboptimal memory construction, information loss, and poor performance, especially for complex memory architectures.
LLMs, particularly smaller ones, struggle to autonomously determine what information to store, how to structure it across different memory components, and when to update it effectively.
results: Mem-α consistently outperformed existing memory-augmented baselines on the MemoryAgentBench, achieving an average performance of 0.592 with a Qwen3-4B backbone, significantly higher than Long-Context (0.461) and MemAgent (0.198).
The framework achieved approximately a 50% reduction in memory footprint compared to baselines like RAG-Top2, demonstrating efficient memory compression while maintaining or improving performance.

Mem-α agents showed robust generalization to sequences exceeding 400k tokens (up to 474K), a more than 13x extrapolation in length from the training data, indicating learned fundamental memory management principles.
solution: Mem-α introduces a reinforcement learning framework that formulates memory construction as a sequential decision-making problem, allowing agents to learn optimal memory operations.
The framework employs a multi-component reward system that optimizes for correctness, tool call format, memory compression, and semantic quality of memory content.
It utilizes a modular and comprehensive memory architecture consisting of distinct core, semantic, and episodic components, each with specialized tools that the RL agent learns to manage.
paper_summary.summary: The Mem-α framework trains large language model agents to autonomously manage complex external memory systems using reinforcement learning, resulting in superior performance and efficient memory construction. This approach enables robust generalization to contexts more than 13 times longer than the training data, while reducing memory footprint by approximately 50%.

title: Mem-α: Learning Memory Construction via Reinforcement Learning

abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) face a crucial challenge from fixed context
windows and inadequate memory management, leading to a severe shortage of
long-term memory capabilities and limited personalization in the interactive
experience with AI agents. To overcome this challenge, we innovatively propose
a Memory Operating System, i.e., MemoryOS, to achieve comprehensive and
efficient memory management for AI agents. Inspired by the memory management
principles in operating systems, MemoryOS designs a hierarchical storage
architecture and consists of four key modules: Memory Storage, Updating,
Retrieval, and Generation. Specifically, the architecture comprises three
levels of storage units: short-term memory, mid-term memory, and long-term
personal memory. Key operations within MemoryOS include dynamic updates between
storage units: short-term to mid-term updates follow a dialogue-chain-based
FIFO principle, while mid-term to long-term updates use a segmented page
organization strategy. Our pioneering MemoryOS enables hierarchical memory
integration and dynamic updating. Extensive experiments on the LoCoMo benchmark
show an average improvement of 49.11% on F1 and 46.18% on BLEU-1 over the
baselines on GPT-4o-mini, showing contextual coherence and personalized memory
retention in long conversations. The implementation code is open-sourced at
this https URL

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

keyInsights: Applying operating system memory management principles, such as hierarchical storage, segmented paging, and heat-based eviction, effectively addresses the long-term memory challenges of AI agents.

A unified and systematic memory management framework, integrating storage, updating, retrieval, and generation, can significantly enhance conversational coherence and personalization beyond isolated memory components.
Mid-Term Memory (MTM) plays a particularly crucial role in maintaining performance in long dialogues, underscoring the importance of a multi-tiered hierarchical design.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) have inherent limitations due to fixed context windows, leading to a lack of effective long-term memory.
AI agents struggle to maintain conversational coherence, consistent persona, and accurate factual recall over extended interactions, often 'forgetting' past information.
Existing memory solutions for LLMs are often fragmented, focusing on specific aspects like knowledge organization, retrieval mechanisms, or architectural modifications in isolation.
results: MemoryOS improved F1 score by 49.11% and BLEU-1 by 46.18% on the LoCoMo benchmark (ultra-long dialogues) when integrated with GPT-4o-mini.
Outperformed previous State-of-the-Art A-Mem on the GVD dataset by 3.2% in Memory Retrieval Accuracy, 5.4% in Response Correctness, and 1.0% in Contextual Coherence.

Achieved higher efficiency by requiring significantly fewer LLM calls (average 4.9 per response vs. 13 for A-Mem*) and substantially lower token consumption during retrieval (3,874 tokens vs. 16,977 for MemGPT).
solution: Introduces MemoryOS, a comprehensive, OS-inspired framework with a three-tier hierarchical memory storage: Short-Term Memory (STM), Mid-Term Memory (MTM), and Long-Term Personal Memory (LPM).
Employs dynamic memory updating mechanisms including FIFO for STM-to-MTM transfers and a 'Heat score' for MTM-to-LPM promotion and eviction, inspired by caching policies.
Utilizes a multi-tiered retrieval module that intelligently fetches relevant information from all three memory levels (STM, MTM, LPM) and synthesizes it for contextually aware response generation.
paper_summary.summary: MemoryOS, developed by researchers at Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications and Tencent AI Lab, proposes a comprehensive operating system-inspired framework to manage long-term memory for AI agents, overcoming LLMs' context window limitations. This system significantly improves conversational coherence and personalization, achieving up to 49.11% F1 score improvement on ultra-long dialogues while reducing LLM calls by over 60% compared to baseline methods.

title: Memory OS of AI Agent

abstract: Agent memory has been touted as a dimension of growth for LLM-based applications, enabling agents that can accumulate experience, adapt across sessions, and move beyond single-shot question answering. The current generation of agent memory systems treats memory as an external layer that extracts salient snippets from conversations, stores them in vector or graph-based stores, and retrieves top-k items into the prompt of an otherwise stateless model. While these systems improve personalization and context carry-over, they still blur the line between evidence and inference, struggle to organize information over long horizons, and offer limited support for agents that must explain their reasoning. We present Hindsight, a memory architecture that treats agent memory as a structured, first-class substrate for reasoning by organizing it into four logical networks that distinguish world facts, agent experiences, synthesized entity summaries, and evolving beliefs. This framework supports three core operations -- retain, recall, and reflect -- that govern how information is added, accessed, and updated. Under this abstraction, a temporal, entity aware memory layer incrementally turns conversational streams into a structured, queryable memory bank, while a reflection layer reasons over this bank to produce answers and to update information in a traceable way. On key long-horizon conversational memory benchmarks like LongMemEval and LoCoMo, Hindsight with an open-source 20B model lifts overall accuracy from 39% to 83.6% over a full-context baseline with the same backbone and outperforms full context GPT-4o. Scaling the backbone further pushes Hindsight to 91.4% on LongMemEval and up to 89.61% on LoCoMo (vs. 75.78% for the strongest prior open system), consistently outperforming existing memory architectures on multi-session and open-domain questions.

keyInsights: Epistemically separating memory into distinct networks for facts, experiences, observations, and evolving opinions provides clarity, traceability, and a robust substrate for advanced reasoning in AI agents.

A multi-strategy retrieval approach, combining vector, keyword, graph-based spreading activation, and temporal search, significantly improves the precision and breadth of memory recall in long-horizon contexts.

Integrating explicit behavioral parameters into an agent's reasoning process enables stable preference consistency and allows for dynamic opinion formation and evolution, moving beyond static persona descriptions.
originalProblem: Existing RAG-based agent memory systems struggle to preserve long-term information and often blur the distinction between observed evidence and inferred beliefs.
Current agents exhibit inconsistent reasoning styles and viewpoints across interactions, failing to maintain a stable persona or track knowledge evolution over extended periods.
Memory systems lack robust support for temporal reasoning and entity-aware knowledge organization, hindering multi-hop discovery and precise information access over long conversational histories.
results: On the LongMemEval benchmark, HINDSIGHT (OSS-20B) achieved 83.6% overall accuracy, a +44.6 percentage point gain over the OSS-20B full-context baseline (39.0%), and outperformed GPT-4o (60.2%) and other memory systems.

HINDSIGHT, when backed by Gemini-3 Pro, achieved the highest overall accuracy of 91.4% on LongMemEval and 89.61% on LoCoMo, demonstrating top-tier performance on multi-session and open-domain question answering.

The system showed substantial improvements in specific long-term categories, increasing multi-session reasoning from 21.1% to 79.7% and temporal reasoning from 31.6% to 79.7% on LongMemEval, confirming its effectiveness against context dilution.
solution: HINDSIGHT employs a four-network memory organization (World, Experience, Opinion, Observation) to epistemically distinguish facts, agent experiences, synthesized entities, and subjective beliefs with confidence scores.

It implements three core operations: Retain (converts conversational streams into structured, linked memories), Recall (retrieves relevant memories using a multi-strategy approach including semantic, keyword, graph, and temporal search), and Reflect (generates responses and updates opinions based on a configurable behavioral profile).
The CARA component integrates a behavioral profile (skepticism, literalism, empathy, bias strength) to modulate system prompts, ensuring preference-conditioned reasoning and consistent agent styles.
paper_summary.summary: HINDSIGHT introduces a novel memory architecture for AI agents, integrating structured memory (TEMPR) with preference-conditioned reasoning (CARA) to enhance long-term recall and behavioral consistency. The system achieves state-of-the-art results on challenging benchmarks, reaching 91.4% accuracy on LongMemEval and 89.61% on LoCoMo, significantly outperforming existing memory systems and full-context baselines, even with open-source LLM backbones.

title: Hindsight is 20/20: Building Agent Memory that Retains, Recalls, and Reflects

abstract: Typical search agents concatenate the entire interaction history into the LLM context, preserving information integrity but producing long, noisy contexts, resulting in high computation and memory costs. In contrast, using only the current turn avoids this overhead but discards essential information. This trade-off limits the scalability of search agents. To address this challenge, we propose MemSearcher, an agent workflow that iteratively maintains a compact memory and combines the current turn with it. At each turn, MemSearcher fuses the user's question with the memory to generate reasoning traces, perform search actions, and update memory to retain only information essential for solving the task. This design stabilizes context length across multi-turn interactions, improving efficiency without sacrificing accuracy. To optimize this workflow, we introduce multi-context GRPO, an end-to-end RL framework that jointly optimize reasoning, search strategies, and memory management of MemSearcher Agents. Specifically, multi-context GRPO samples groups of trajectories under different contexts and propagates trajectory-level advantages across all conversations within them. Trained on the same dataset as Search-R1, MemSearcher achieves significant improvements over strong baselines on seven public benchmarks: +11% on Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct and +12% on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct relative average gains. Notably, the 3B-based MemSearcher even outperforms 7B-based baselines, demonstrating that striking a balance between information integrity and efficiency yields both higher accuracy and lower computational overhead. The code and models will be publicly available at this https URL

keyInsights: Intelligent, LLM-driven memory management can effectively stabilize context length and prevent the linear growth observed in ReAct-based agents.
End-to-end reinforcement learning is crucial for equipping LLMs with the complex abilities to simultaneously interact with search engines and effectively manage memory.
Architectural and algorithmic innovations like efficient context management can enable smaller LLMs to outperform larger models using less efficient paradigms.
originalProblem: ReAct-based LLM agents suffer from unbounded context growth, leading to prohibitive computational and memory costs for multi-turn interactions.
The increasing context length in conventional agents causes performance degradation and limits scalability in real-world applications.
Existing methods struggle to efficiently manage long interaction histories while preserving essential information for accurate problem-solving.
results: Achieved average relative performance gains of 11% (Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct) and 12% (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) across seven diverse knowledge-acquisition benchmarks.
Demonstrated superior efficiency with substantially lower and nearly constant token counts, leading to reduced and more stable peak GPU memory usage compared to ReAct-based baselines.
The 3B-parameter MemSearcher agent outperformed several 7B-parameter baseline methods, indicating a more effective utilization of model capacity through intelligent memory management.
solution: An iterative memory management workflow where the LLM dynamically condenses previous interaction history into a compact, fixed-length memory at each turn.

A multi-context Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm is employed for end-to-end reinforcement learning, enabling the LLM to learn reasoning, searching, and memory management simultaneously.
The LLM itself acts as a memory manager, processing observations and prior memory to generate an updated memory, constrained to a maximum token length (e.g., 1,024 tokens).
paper_summary.summary: MEMSEARCHER enhances LLM-based search agents by introducing an iterative memory management workflow trained with end-to-end reinforcement learning. This approach achieves superior performance on knowledge-acquisition benchmarks while significantly reducing token counts and GPU memory usage compared to existing ReAct paradigms.

title: MemSearcher: Training LLMs to Reason, Search and Manage Memory via End-to-End Reinforcement Learning

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as long-term interactive agents, yet their limited context windows make it difficult to sustain coherent behavior over extended interactions. Existing memory systems often store isolated records and retrieve fragments, limiting their ability to consolidate evolving user states and resolve conflicts. We introduce EverMemOS, a self-organizing memory operating system that implements an engram-inspired lifecycle for computational memory. Episodic Trace Formation converts dialogue streams into MemCells that capture episodic traces, atomic facts, and time-bounded Foresight signals. Semantic Consolidation organizes MemCells into thematic MemScenes, distilling stable semantic structures and updating user profiles. Reconstructive Recollection performs MemScene-guided agentic retrieval to compose the necessary and sufficient context for downstream reasoning. Experiments on LoCoMo and LongMemEval show that EverMemOS achieves state-of-the-art performance on memory-augmented reasoning tasks. We further report a profile study on PersonaMem v2 and qualitative case studies illustrating chat-oriented capabilities such as user profiling and Foresight. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Modeling computational memory after the biological engram lifecycle (trace formation, consolidation, recollection) enables agents to organize fragmented experiences into coherent, stable knowledge structures, moving beyond simple storage and retrieval.

The explicit hierarchical organization into MemCells (stable semantic units) and MemScenes (thematic organizational units), alongside a distinct User Profile, facilitates robust multi-hop reasoning and consistent user modeling over long horizons.

Integrating foresight signals with validity intervals and using agentic context verification allows for proactive decision-making and efficient, task-specific context composition, which minimizes noise and ensures necessary information is retrieved.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with coherent, long-horizon reasoning due primarily to constrained context windows and phenomena like "Lost-in-the-Middle."

Existing memory systems often treat memories as isolated records, failing to consolidate fragmented episodic experiences into stable, higher-level knowledge structures, which leads to inconsistent agent behavior.
Current approaches lack a systematic, lifecycle-driven process for memory formation, organization, and retrieval, hindering the dynamic evolution of an agent's knowledge over extended interactions.

Many memory systems do not effectively distinguish between temporary states and enduring traits, leading to difficulties in maintaining stable user profiles or making proactive, experience-grounded decisions.
results: EverMemOS achieved an overall accuracy of 93.05% on the LoCoMo benchmark using a GPT-4.1-mini backbone, representing a 9.2% relative improvement over the strongest baseline (Zep at 85.22%).
The system demonstrated substantial relative improvements in complex reasoning, showing a 19.7% gain in multi-hop questions on LoCoMo and a 20.6% gain in knowledge update tasks on LongMemEval.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contribution of both MemCells and MemScenes, with stepwise performance degradation observed upon their removal, and validated the effectiveness of LLM-based semantic segmentation over fixed-heuristic chunking.
The inclusion of a consolidated User Profile derived from semantic consolidation provided a 9.32 percentage point improvement in overall accuracy on PersonaMem-v2 compared to using episodes-only.
solution: EverMemOS introduces a self-organizing memory operating system that models computational memory as a dynamic, three-phase lifecycle: Episodic Trace Formation, Semantic Consolidation, and Reconstructive Recollection.

Episodic Trace Formation converts interaction history into stable MemCells, which encapsulate an episode narrative, atomic facts, and time-valid foresight signals using LLM-mediated segmentation and synthesis.

Semantic Consolidation incrementally clusters new MemCells into higher-order thematic MemScenes and updates a compact User Profile, transforming transient episodic traces into stable, consolidated knowledge.

Reconstructive Recollection implements an agentic retrieval process guided by necessity and sufficiency, where relevant MemScenes and time-valid Foresight are selected and composed, with an LLM verifying context sufficiency and rewriting queries if needed.
paper_summary.summary: EverMemOS introduces a self-organizing memory operating system for LLM agents, inspired by biological engram lifecycles, that transforms fragmented interactions into structured knowledge. This enables coherent and consistent long-horizon reasoning, achieving up to a 9.2% relative accuracy improvement on the LoCoMo benchmark and significant gains in complex reasoning tasks.

title: EverMemOS: A Self-Organizing Memory Operating System for Structured Long-Horizon Reasoning

abstract: Memory-Augmented Generation (MAG) extends Large Language Models with external memory to support long-context reasoning, but existing approaches largely rely on semantic similarity over monolithic memory stores, entangling temporal, causal, and entity information. This design limits interpretability and alignment between query intent and retrieved evidence, leading to suboptimal reasoning accuracy. In this paper, we propose MAGMA, a multi-graph agentic memory architecture that represents each memory item across orthogonal semantic, temporal, causal, and entity graphs. MAGMA formulates retrieval as policy-guided traversal over these relational views, enabling query-adaptive selection and structured context construction. By decoupling memory representation from retrieval logic, MAGMA provides transparent reasoning paths and fine-grained control over retrieval. Experiments on LoCoMo and LongMemEval demonstrate that MAGMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art agentic memory systems in long-horizon reasoning tasks.

keyInsights: Explicitly disentangling and modeling diverse relational dimensions in a multi-graph structure significantly improves an agent's ability to reason over long-term contexts.
Adaptive, intent-aware retrieval policies that leverage distinct relational graphs lead to more precise and interpretable information retrieval compared to monolithic semantic search.
A dual-stream memory evolution, combining fast ingestion with asynchronous structural consolidation, is crucial for maintaining agent responsiveness while building rich, queryable long-term memory.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle with long-term context, consistent identity, and complex reasoning over extended interactions due to finite attention windows and context decay.
Existing Memory-Augmented Generation (MAG) systems rely on monolithic memory stores and primary semantic similarity, entangling temporal, causal, and entity information.
This monolithic approach hinders interpretability, reduces alignment between query intent and retrieved evidence, and leads to suboptimal reasoning, particularly for complex 'why' questions.
results: Achieved the highest overall Judge score of 0.700 on the LoCoMo benchmark, representing an 18.6% to 45.5% improvement over state-of-the-art baselines.

Demonstrated superior performance in reasoning-intensive tasks, scoring 0.650 for temporal reasoning and 0.742 under adversarial conditions, validating the robustness of its adaptive traversal policy.

Reduced token consumption by over 95% on the LongMemEval benchmark (0.7k-4.2k tokens/query vs. >100k for full-context), while achieving 61.2% accuracy and the lowest average query latency of 1.47 seconds (40% faster).
solution: Introduces a multi-graph architecture where distinct relational dimensions (temporal, causal, semantic, entity) are explicitly modeled as separate, orthogonal graphs within a unified time-variant memory substrate.
Develops an adaptive hierarchical retrieval process that analyzes query intent and employs a policy-guided graph traversal to dynamically select and navigate relevant relational paths.
Utilizes a dual-stream memory evolution system, including a fast path for immediate event ingestion and a slow path for asynchronous, LLM-driven structural consolidation of causal and entity links.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at UT Dallas and the University of Florida introduced MAGMA, a multi-graph agentic memory architecture that models semantic, temporal, causal, and entity relations as distinct graphs. This system achieved up to 45.5% higher reasoning accuracy on long-context benchmarks while reducing token consumption by over 95% and demonstrating 40% faster query latency compared to prior methods.

title: MAGMA: A Multi-Graph based Agentic Memory Architecture for AI Agents

abstract: Memory systems have been designed to leverage past experiences in Large Language Model (LLM) agents. However, many deployed memory systems primarily optimize compression and storage, with comparatively less emphasis on explicit, closed-loop control of memory retrieval. From this observation, we build memory retrieval as an autonomous, accurate, and compatible agent system, named MemR$^3$, which has two core mechanisms: 1) a router that selects among retrieve, reflect, and answer actions to optimize answer quality; 2) a global evidence-gap tracker that explicitly renders the answering process transparent and tracks the evidence collection process. This design departs from the standard retrieve-then-answer pipeline by introducing a closed-loop control mechanism that enables autonomous decision-making. Empirical results on the LoCoMo benchmark demonstrate that MemR$^3$ surpasses strong baselines on LLM-as-a-Judge score, and particularly, it improves existing retrievers across four categories with an overall improvement on RAG (+7.29%) and Zep (+1.94%) using GPT-4.1-mini backend, offering a plug-and-play controller for existing memory stores.

keyInsights: Maintaining an explicit "evidence-gap" state allows agents to reason under partial observability and dynamically refine their information-seeking strategy.
Transforming memory retrieval into a closed-loop, autonomous sequential decision process significantly enhances the accuracy and efficiency of LLM agents.
A plug-and-play retrieval controller can be integrated with diverse memory backends (e.g., RAG, Zep) to improve retrieval quality without modifying storage architecture.
originalProblem: Non-parametric memory systems for LLM agents often rely on heuristic, open-loop retrieval, leading to noisy recall, excessive context, and latency issues.
LLM agents lack explicit, closed-loop control mechanisms for memory retrieval, limiting their ability to intelligently gather specific information.
Managing and leveraging long-term, personalized memories remains a significant challenge for sophisticated AI agents.
results: MemR^3 improved LLM-as-a-Judge (J) scores by up to 7.29% (RAG-backed, GPT-4.1-mini) on the LoCoMo benchmark, consistently outperforming its base retrievers and baselines.
The system demonstrated substantial gains across multi-hop, temporal, and open-domain questions, approaching or surpassing full-context performance in several categories.
Ablation studies highlighted the crucial role of "masked retrieval" (preventing redundant retrieval) and the positive impact of explicit reflection and query refinement.
solution: MemR^3 frames memory retrieval as an autonomous, sequential decision process within a LangGraph-based agent graph.
It introduces a global evidence-gap tracker (E, G) to explicitly monitor known facts and remaining informational needs, guiding retrieval.
A Router node provides closed-loop control, dynamically orchestrating actions between Retrieve, Reflect, and Answer nodes to refine knowledge.
paper_summary.summary: MemR^3 introduces a system for LLM agents that uses reflective reasoning and an explicit evidence-gap tracker to enable closed-loop control over memory retrieval. This approach consistently enhanced answer quality, achieving up to a 7.29% improvement in LLM-as-a-Judge scores on the LoCoMo benchmark compared to traditional RAG.

title: MemR$^3$: Memory Retrieval via Reflective Reasoning for LLM Agents

abstract: Long-term memory is one of the key factors influencing the reasoning capabilities of Large Language Model Agents (LLM Agents). Incorporating a memory mechanism that effectively integrates past interactions can significantly enhance decision-making and contextual coherence of LLM Agents. While recent works have made progress in memory storage and retrieval, such as encoding memory into dense vectors for similarity-based search or organizing knowledge in the form of graph, these approaches often fall short in structured memory organization and efficient retrieval. To address these limitations, we propose a Hierarchical Memory (H-MEM) architecture for LLM Agents that organizes and updates memory in a multi-level fashion based on the degree of semantic abstraction. Each memory vector is embedded with a positional index encoding pointing to its semantically related sub-memories in the next layer. During the reasoning phase, an index-based routing mechanism enables efficient, layer-by-layer retrieval without performing exhaustive similarity computations. We evaluate our method on five task settings from the LoCoMo dataset. Experimental results show that our approach consistently outperforms five baseline methods, demonstrating its effectiveness in long-term dialogue scenarios.

keyInsights: Structuring memory hierarchically with embedded positional indices enables a targeted retrieval process that significantly reduces the search space and computational complexity compared to flat vector storage.

Dynamic adjustment of memory strength, incorporating user feedback, allows the memory system to adapt to evolving conversational contexts and user interests, reinforcing relevant memories and fading irrelevant ones.

The synergy between hierarchical organization and index-based retrieval is critical for scalable and efficient long-term reasoning in LLM agents, offering a robust alternative to purely vector-based or complex graph-based memory systems.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from limited context windows, making it challenging to maintain coherence and context over extended, multi-turn interactions.

Existing memory mechanisms for LLM agents often rely on inefficient retrieval methods, such as exhaustive similarity searches across a growing memory store, leading to high computational costs and slow inference.

Many current memory systems lack a structured organization, hindering precise information localization and integration, and use simplistic update mechanisms that do not adapt to dynamic user interests or feedback.
results: H-MEM consistently achieved superior performance, demonstrating average F1 score improvements of 14.98 points and BLEU-1 score improvements of 12.77 points over baseline methods on the LoCoMo dataset.

The architecture exhibited significant computational efficiency, maintaining inference times below 100 ms even at maximum memory load, which is up to 5 times faster than vector-based baselines like MemoryBank.

H-MEM showed particularly strong performance in complex tasks, outperforming baselines by 21.25 points in F1 for Multi-Hop questions and 16.71 points in F1 for Adversarial tasks, maintaining effectiveness across various LLM scales.
solution: H-MEM introduces a four-level hierarchical memory architecture (Domain, Category, Memory Trace, Episode Layers) that semantically organizes information, mirroring human cognitive processes.

An index-based routing mechanism is implemented for memory retrieval, where each memory entry includes positional indices pointing to related sub-memories, enabling efficient, top-down traversal without exhaustive searches.

A dynamic memory update mechanism adjusts memory strength based on LLM-generated feedback weights and explicit user feedback (approval, no feedback, rebuttal), enhancing adaptability beyond traditional forgetting curves.
paper_summary.summary: An H-MEM architecture enables Large Language Model agents to achieve high-efficiency long-term reasoning through a novel hierarchical memory structure and index-based retrieval mechanism. This system demonstrably improves F1 and BLEU-1 scores on complex long-term dialogue tasks while drastically reducing computational overhead compared to existing memory approaches.

title: Hierarchical Memory for High-Efficiency Long-Term Reasoning in LLM Agents

abstract: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) have enabled conversational AI agents to engage in extended multi-turn interactions spanning weeks or months. However, existing memory systems struggle to reason over temporally grounded facts and preferences that evolve across months of interaction and lack effective retrieval strategies for multi-hop, time-sensitive queries over long dialogue histories. We introduce Chronos, a novel temporal-aware memory framework that decomposes raw dialogue into subject-verb-object event tuples with resolved datetime ranges and entity aliases, indexing them in a structured event calendar alongside a turn calendar that preserves full conversational context. At query time, Chronos applies dynamic prompting to generate tailored retrieval guidance for each question, directing the agent on what to retrieve, how to filter across time ranges, and how to approach multi-hop reasoning through an iterative tool-calling loop over both calendars. We evaluate Chronos with 8 LLMs, both open-source and closed-source, on the LongMemEvalS benchmark comprising 500 questions spanning six categories of dialogue history tasks. Chronos Low achieves 92.60% and Chronos High scores 95.60% accuracy, setting a new state of the art with an improvement of 7.67% over the best prior system. Ablation results reveal the events calendar accounts for a 58.9% gain on the baseline while all other components yield improvements between 15.5% and 22.3%. Notably, Chronos Low alone surpasses prior approaches evaluated under their strongest model configurations.

keyInsights: Structuring temporal events with multi-resolution normalization significantly enhances conversational agents' ability to perform multi-session aggregation and temporal reasoning.
Query-conditioned dynamic prompting can effectively guide retrieval strategies, optimizing an LLM agent's access to relevant memory components.

A hybrid memory approach, combining structured event calendars with raw turn calendars, coupled with an agentic iterative retrieval process, provides a balanced and robust solution for long-term conversational memory.
originalProblem: Existing conversational memory systems struggle to effectively manage and retrieve long-term, temporally grounded information, particularly for evolving facts and multi-hop queries.
Current approaches either over-structure knowledge (e.g., knowledge graphs leading to context entropy) or lack the necessary temporal depth (e.g., simple turn-level retrieval).
Large Language Models (LLMs) face inherent difficulties in precisely handling and reasoning about temporal relationships and calculations from unstructured text.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on the LongMemEvalS benchmark, with Chronos High (Claude Opus 4.6) reaching 95.60% accuracy, a 3.02% relative improvement over prior systems.

Demonstrated strong accuracy across various categories, particularly in temporal reasoning (90.23%), multi-session aggregation (91.73%), and knowledge-update tracking (96.15%) with Chronos Low (GPT-4o).

Ablation studies confirmed the event calendar's crucial role, with its removal causing a 34.5 percentage point accuracy drop for Chronos Low, and highlighted the benefits of dynamic prompting and hybrid search strategies.
solution: Introduces Chronos, a memory framework that selectively extracts temporally grounded events into an 'event calendar' while preserving full conversational context in a 'turn calendar'.
Employs an LLM-powered event extraction pipeline that converts natural language time references into precise, multi-resolution ISO 8601 datetime ranges.
Utilizes dynamic prompting to generate query-specific retrieval guidance and an LLM agent with tool-calling to iteratively search and combine information from both structured events and raw turns.
paper_summary.summary: Chronos is a temporal-aware memory framework for conversational AI, designed to enhance reasoning over temporally grounded facts and preferences across long, multi-turn interactions. It achieved a state-of-the-art accuracy of 95.60% on the LongMemEvalS benchmark, improving performance by 3.02% relative to prior systems.

title: Chronos: Temporal-Aware Conversational Agents with Structured Event Retrieval for Long-Term Memory

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly rely on external memory to support long-horizon interaction, personalized assistance, and multi-step reasoning. However, existing memory systems still face three core challenges: they often rely too heavily on semantic similarity, which can miss evidence crucial for user-centric understanding; they frequently store related experiences as isolated fragments, weakening temporal and causal coherence; and they typically use static memory granularities that do not adapt well to the requirements of different questions. We propose AdaMem, an adaptive user-centric memory framework for long-horizon dialogue agents. AdaMem organizes dialogue history into working, episodic, persona, and graph memories, enabling the system to preserve recent context, structured long-term experiences, stable user traits, and relation-aware connections within a unified framework. At inference time, AdaMem first resolves the target participant, then builds a question-conditioned retrieval route that combines semantic retrieval with relation-aware graph expansion only when needed, and finally produces the answer through a role-specialized pipeline for evidence synthesis and response generation. We evaluate AdaMem on the LoCoMo and PERSONAMEM benchmarks for long-horizon reasoning and user modeling. Experimental results show that AdaMem achieves state-of-the-art performance on both benchmarks. The code will be released upon acceptance.

keyInsights: Structuring memory into participant-specific, heterogeneous types (working, episodic, persona, graph) enables more comprehensive user-centric understanding beyond simple semantic matching.

Adaptive, question-conditioned retrieval, which considers temporal, relational, and attribute cues, can significantly enhance the precision and completeness of information recovery for complex reasoning tasks.
Fusing evidence from multiple memory sources and employing a multi-agent pipeline for synthesis improves overall answer quality and reasoning robustness in long-horizon dialogues.
originalProblem: Existing memory systems over-rely on semantic similarity for retrieval, often missing contextually relevant user preferences or attributes vital for comprehensive understanding.
Fragmented storage of related experiences weakens temporal and causal coherence, making it difficult for agents to reconstruct events or understand dependencies.
Static memory granularities are suboptimal for long-horizon reasoning, as overly coarse memories introduce noise and overly fine-grained fragments obscure cross-event dependencies.
results: AdaMem achieved state-of-the-art performance on the LoCoMo benchmark with GPT-4.1-mini, showing a +4.4% relative F1 improvement overall and a +23.4% F1 gain in the temporal question category.
It outperformed all baselines on the PERSONAMEM benchmark with a 5.9% average accuracy increase, demonstrating a 27.3% relative improvement on the 'generalize to new scenarios' task.

Ablation studies confirmed the complementary roles of AdaMem's components, with graph memory contributing the largest performance gain, underscoring the importance of relation-aware memory for cross-turn dependencies.
solution: AdaMem establishes a memory-augmented dialogue framework comprising participant-specific working, episodic, persona, and graph memories for structured information storage.
It employs a multi-agent collaboration pipeline for online memory construction (message understanding, consolidation, topic regrouping) and question-conditioned retrieval.
Adaptive retrieval planning dynamically selects graph expansion, propagation depth, and evidence fusion strategies based on question cues to combine semantic and relation-aware evidence.
paper_summary.summary: AdaMem introduces an adaptive user-centric memory framework for long-horizon dialogue agents, structuring conversational history into participant-specific working, episodic, persona, and graph memories. This system achieves state-of-the-art performance, with a +4.4% relative F1 improvement on the LoCoMo benchmark and a 5.9% average accuracy increase on PERSONAMEM, by dynamically adapting retrieval and synthesis to question cues.

title: AdaMem: Adaptive User-Centric Memory for Long-Horizon Dialogue Agents

abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used for long-duration tasks, maintaining effective long-term memory has become a critical challenge. Current methods often face a trade-off between cost and accuracy. Simple storage methods often fail to retrieve relevant information, while complex indexing methods (such as memory graphs) require heavy computation and can cause information loss. Furthermore, relying on the working LLM to process all memories is computationally expensive and slow. To address these limitations, we propose MemSifter, a novel framework that offloads the memory retrieval process to a small-scale proxy model. Instead of increasing the burden on the primary working LLM, MemSifter uses a smaller model to reason about the task before retrieving the necessary information. This approach requires no heavy computation during the indexing phase and adds minimal overhead during inference. To optimize the proxy model, we introduce a memory-specific Reinforcement Learning (RL) training paradigm. We design a task-outcome-oriented reward based on the working LLM's actual performance in completing the task. The reward measures the actual contribution of retrieved memories by mutiple interactions with the working LLM, and discriminates retrieved rankings by stepped decreasing contributions. Additionally, we employ training techniques such as Curriculum Learning and Model Merging to improve performance. We evaluated MemSifter on eight LLM memory benchmarks, including Deep Research tasks. The results demonstrate that our method meets or exceeds the performance of existing state-of-the-art approaches in both retrieval accuracy and final task completion. MemSifter offers an efficient and scalable solution for long-term LLM memory. We have open-sourced the model weights, code, and training data to support further research.

keyInsights: Offloading complex memory reasoning and retrieval to a lightweight proxy model provides an efficient and scalable solution, allowing the main LLM to focus on core task execution.
Optimizing retrieval directly based on the working LLM's task outcome through RL, rather than intermediate metrics, leads to superior downstream performance by aligning retrieval with true utility.

Reward components like marginal utility (against a no-memory baseline) and rank-sensitive weighting are crucial for addressing ambiguous credit assignment and ensuring critical information is prioritized in retrieved contexts.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) are bottlenecked by context window limitations and high computational costs when required to leverage vast, long-term interaction histories.
Existing memory retrieval systems face a cost-accuracy trade-off, either being computationally intensive for indexing or providing noisy, irrelevant context.

Traditional retrieval optimization methods, often based on isolated metrics or static relevance labels, do not directly align with the working LLM's final task success in complex, long-horizon reasoning.
results: MemSifter consistently achieved higher F1 scores across eight diverse LLM memory benchmarks, outperforming embedding-based methods, generative rerankers, and complex memory frameworks.
It matched or exceeded the performance of powerful long-context LLMs while significantly reducing computational overhead, offering an efficient alternative for extensive histories.

Ablation studies confirmed that the task-outcome-oriented reward, marginal utility, and rank-sensitive weighting are indispensable for the system's improved retrieval quality and end-to-end task performance.
solution: A lightweight, specialized proxy model is introduced to offload memory retrieval and reasoning from the primary working LLM.
The proxy model performs 'Think-and-Rank' inference, generating reasoning rationales and predicting the top-k most relevant memory segments from pre-filtered historical sessions.
A novel Reinforcement Learning (RL) paradigm trains the proxy model by directly optimizing for the working LLM's final task outcome, utilizing marginal utility and rank-sensitive reward signals.
paper_summary.summary: A lightweight proxy model handles long-term memory retrieval and reasoning for large language models, employing an outcome-driven reinforcement learning paradigm to enhance efficiency. This approach demonstrates improved retrieval accuracy and overall task performance across various benchmarks for LLMs performing long-duration tasks.

title: MemSifter: Offloading LLM Memory Retrieval via Outcome-Driven Proxy Reasoning

abstract:
Memory plays a key role in enhancing LLMs' performance when deployed to
real-world applications. Existing solutions face trade-offs: explicit memory
designs based on external storage require complex management and incur storage
overhead, while implicit memory designs that store information via parameters
struggle with reliable retrieval. In this paper, we propose R$^3$Mem, a memory
network that optimizes both information Retention and Retrieval through
Reversible context compression. Specifically, R$^3$Mem employs virtual memory
tokens to compress and encode infinitely long histories, further enhanced by a
hierarchical compression strategy that refines information from document- to
entity-level for improved assimilation across granularities. For retrieval,
R$^3$Mem employs a reversible architecture, reconstructing raw data by invoking
the model backward with compressed information. Implemented via
parameter-efficient fine-tuning, it can integrate seamlessly with any
Transformer-based model. Experiments demonstrate that our memory design
achieves state-of-the-art performance in long-context language modeling and
retrieval-augmented generation tasks. It also significantly outperforms
conventional memory modules in long-horizon interaction tasks like
conversational agents, showcasing its potential for next-generation retrieval
systems.

keyInsights: Treating memory retrieval as the inverse operation of memory retention (compression) allows for a unified, reliable memory system within LLMs.
Hierarchical encoding of information at multiple granularities (document, paragraph, sentence, entity) significantly improves retention and enables flexible retrieval.
Enforcing cycle consistency during training is crucial for ensuring that implicitly stored information can be reliably and accurately reconstructed.
originalProblem: Large Language Models are stateless and have fixed context windows, limiting their ability to leverage past experiences or maintain coherence in long interactions.
Existing explicit memory systems often face challenges with complex memory management, significant storage overhead, and retrieval complexity.
